days.title: Days of the week

vocab.exampleEn: Wednesday is the middle of the week.
On Thursday I have a lesson.

grammar.title: The seven days of the week
grammar.explanation: Russian has seven days of the week. Note that the week **starts on Monday**, not Sunday. Three of the day names are **feminine** (`среда`, `пятница`, `суббота`, `неделя`) and the others are **masculine** (`понедельник`, `вторник`, `четверг`) or **neuter** (`воскресенье`). The days are **not capitalized** in Russian. To ask what day it is, say `Какой сегодня день?` (literally 'Which today day?').

reading.title: Какой день?
reading.text: — Какой сегодня день? — Сегодня пятница. — Хорошо! Завтра суббота.
reading.translation: — What day is it today? — Today is Friday. — Good! Tomorrow is Saturday.

days.speakingPrompt: Say all seven days of the week out loud in order. Then say what day it is today and what day tomorrow is, using «Сегодня…» and «Завтра…».

days.title: Telling the hour (o'clock)

grammar.title: Telling time: full hours
grammar.explanation: To say the hour, use the number plus the right form of `час` (hour). The form changes with the number: **1** → `час` (`час` alone, no number needed); **2, 3, 4** → `часа`; **5–12** → `часов`. To ask the time, say `Который час?` or `Сколько сейчас времени?` This pattern (counting form after numbers) is the same one you met when counting objects.

examples.english: What time is it? — Eight o'clock.

reading.title: Который час?
reading.text: — Доброе утро! Который час? — Сейчас ровно семь часов. — Спасибо!
reading.translation: — Good morning! What time is it? — It is exactly seven o'clock. — Thank you!

days.speakingPrompt: Practice saying the full hours from one to twelve, choosing the correct form: час / часа / часов. Then ask «Который час?» and answer for three different times.

days.title: Half hours and minutes (rough time)

vocab.exampleEn: It is half past four now.

grammar.title: Approximate time and 'half'
grammar.explanation: For now we keep time simple and natural. Use `минута` for minutes: **2, 3, 4** → `минуты`, **5 and up** → `минут`. To talk about midday and midnight just use the single words `полдень` and `полночь`. The adverbs `почти` (almost) and `уже` (already) make the time approximate: `почти три часа` = almost three o'clock. You will learn the full 'half past' and 'quarter' system later; for now `половина пятого` is a useful fixed phrase meaning 'half past four' (literally 'half of the fifth').

examples.english: It is almost nine o'clock already.

reading.title: Почти полдень
reading.text: — Который час? — Уже почти полдень. — Хорошо, у нас есть пять минут.
reading.translation: — What time is it? — It is almost noon already. — Good, we have five minutes.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe the current time approximately using «почти» and «уже» (for example «уже почти час»). Then say how many minutes you have before your next task, choosing минута / минуты / минут correctly.

days.title: Saying when: 'on' a day and 'at' an hour

vocab.exampleEn: I have a meeting on Wednesday.
The lesson is at two o'clock.

grammar.title: «в» for days and hours
grammar.explanation: Use the preposition `в` to say **when** something happens. With a day of the week, `в` takes the **accusative** case — and for these day nouns the accusative looks almost like the dictionary form, except feminine `-а` becomes `-у`: `среда → в среду`, `пятница → в пятницу`, `суббота → в субботу`. The masculine/neuter days don't change: `в понедельник`, `в четверг`, `в воскресенье`. With a clock time, `в` plus the hour means 'at': `в два часа` = at two o'clock.

examples.english: The lesson is at three o'clock.
On Saturday is my day off.

reading.title: Когда встреча?
reading.text: — Когда у тебя встреча? — В среду, в два часа. — А обед? — Обед в час.
reading.translation: — When is your meeting? — On Wednesday, at two o'clock. — And lunch? — Lunch is at one o'clock.

days.speakingPrompt: Answer the question «Когда?» for three events (work, lunch, a meeting). Use «в + day» (remember в среду / в пятницу / в субботу) and «в + hour» together, e.g. «Встреча в пятницу, в три часа».

days.title: My daily schedule

vocab.exampleEn: I have breakfast at seven o'clock.
We have lunch at one o'clock.
They have dinner in the evening.

grammar.title: Daily-routine verbs + the accusative object
grammar.explanation: Routine verbs like `начинать` and `заканчивать` take a **direct object in the accusative**. For inanimate feminine nouns the accusative ending is `-у`: `работа → работу`, `встреча → встречу`. Masculine inanimate nouns and neuter nouns keep their dictionary form: `урок → урок`, `обед → обед`. So `Я начинаю работу` (I start work) and `Я заканчиваю урок` (I finish the lesson). `каждый день` (every day) is a handy fixed phrase for routines.

examples.russian: Я начинаю работу в девять часов.
examples.english: I start work at nine o'clock.

examples.russian: Каждый день я завтракаю в семь.
examples.english: Every day I have breakfast at seven.
We finish the meeting at three.

reading.title: Моё расписание
reading.text: Каждый день я завтракаю в семь часов. Я начинаю работу в девять и заканчиваю в пять. Вечером я ужинаю.
reading.translation: Every day I have breakfast at seven o'clock. I start work at nine and finish at five. In the evening I have dinner.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe your own daily schedule in 4–5 sentences. Say when you have breakfast, start and finish work, and have dinner, using «в + hour» and «каждый день». Remember the accusative «-у» on «работу» and «встречу».

days.title: Putting it together: a weekly plan

vocab.example: У меня есть план на неделю.
vocab.exampleEn: I have a plan for the week.
I usually work during the day.

grammar.title: Sequencing a week: days + times + accusative together
grammar.explanation: Now combine everything: a day with `в` (`в среду`), a time with `в` (`в два часа`), an accusative object (`встречу`, `работу`), and sequence words `сначала` (first), `потом` (then), `обычно` (usually). To say you are 'free' or 'busy' as a predicate, Russian uses the **short form** of the adjective: `свободный → свободен` (свободна for a woman), `занятый → занят` (занята for a woman). Word order is flexible, but a natural pattern is **time expression first, then subject, then verb, then object**: `В пятницу я начинаю работу рано.` The adverbs `рано` / `поздно` describe when, and `обычно` marks a routine.

examples.english: Usually on Monday I am busy.

examples.russian: Сначала я завтракаю, потом начинаю работу.
examples.english: First I have breakfast, then I start work.

reading.title: Мой план на неделю
reading.text: В понедельник и в среду у меня встреча в два часа. В пятницу я работаю рано. А в субботу я свободен — это мой выходной.
reading.translation: On Monday and Wednesday I have a meeting at two o'clock. On Friday I work early. And on Saturday I am free — that is my day off.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe your plan for the whole week. For at least three days say «в + day» what you do and «в + hour» when, and mark whether you are «свободен» or «занят». Use «сначала… потом…» at least once.

days.title: Review: time & schedule

grammar.title: Week 13 recap: time, days, and the accusative
grammar.explanation: This week you learned to **tell time** (`Который час?` → `час / часа / часов`), name the **seven days** (Monday-start, not capitalized), and say **when** with `в`: `в среду`, `в пятницу`, `в субботу` (feminine days take accusative `-у`; masculine/neuter days stay the same) and `в два часа` for hours. You reviewed the **accusative direct object**: feminine inanimate nouns end in `-у` (`работу`, `встречу`), while masculine inanimate and neuter nouns keep their form (`урок`, `обед`). To say you are free or busy, use the short forms `свободен` / `занят`. Sequence your day with `сначала`, `потом`, `обычно`, `рано`, `поздно`.

examples.russian: Который час? — Уже почти пять часов.
examples.english: What time is it? — It is almost five o'clock already.

examples.russian: В пятницу я начинаю работу в девять.
examples.english: On Friday I start work at nine.

reading.title: Какой у тебя план?
reading.text: — Когда у тебя встреча? — В среду, в два часа. — А в субботу? — В субботу я свободен. Обычно я отдыхаю и ужинаю поздно. — Хорошо, тогда суббота — наш выходной!
reading.translation: — When is your meeting? — On Wednesday, at two o'clock. — And on Saturday? — On Saturday I am free. Usually I rest and have dinner late. — Good, then Saturday is our day off!

days.speakingPrompt: Hold a short conversation with yourself: ask «Который час?» and answer; ask «Когда у тебя встреча?» and answer with a day and an hour; then say one thing you do on your day off. Use this week's days, hour forms, «в + day/hour», and the short form «свободен» / «занят».
